Two recent surveys provide comprehensive taxonomies of memory
Type Task Goal Scenario Goal in LLM-based agents. Zhang et al. [17] organize the design space
Aggregate 91.1 83.3 along three dimensions—memory sources (agent-environment inDifficulty 1 94.4 83.3 teractions, internal reasoning, user feedback), memory forms (natuDifficulty 2 88.9 83.3 ral language, embeddings, databases, structured knowledge), and
Difficulty 3 88.9 83.3 memory operations (read, write, reflect, manage)—and identify key
limitations of existing work: overly simplistic representations, unsophisticated operations for deciding what to remember or forget,
Table 9: Baseline Agent (No Memory): Train and fragmented evaluation. Du et al. [4] take a complementary
operations-centric view, defining six atomic memory operations:
Type Task Goal Scenario Goal consolidation, updating, indexing, forgetting, retrieval, and comAggregate 86.7 73.3 pression. In their vocabulary, our tip extraction constitutes a form
Difficulty 1 100.0 100.0 of consolidation (converting raw trajectories into abstract tips), tip
Difficulty 2 77.8 58.3 refinement is updating, and selective retention is forgetting. Both
Difficulty 3 77.8 50.0 surveys note that most existing systems store raw or lightly processed text, lacking the structured abstraction and quality-aware
curation that effective agent memory requires. Our framework
Table 10: Subtask Tips + LLM Selection: Dev directly addresses these identified gaps.
